places.name: Alma

visits.text: Hopewell Rocks at Hopewell Cape: more than twenty sandstone and conglomerate columns crowned with trees rise along 2 km of Bay of Fundy coastline. Nicknamed "flowerpots" for the trees growing atop them, these formations result from millions of years of erosion by the world's highest tides. The 350-million-year-old rock layers were tilted 30-45° by tectonic activity, then sculpted by water. According to Mi'kmaq legend, these pillars are slaves turned to stone by angry whales as they attempted to escape. At low tide, you walk at the base of the columns; six hours later, they're submerged under 10 to 12 metres of water.

Fundy National Park (207 km², created 1948): the park's coastline experiences the full Bay of Fundy tidal cycle—15 metres difference between high and low tide. At low tide, the ocean floor transforms into exploration terrain, with seaweed, periwinkles, and starfish clinging to rocks. The park also harbours over 25 waterfalls in steep valleys cloaked in Acadian forest.

Hike to Dickson Falls, Fundy Park's most photographed waterfall: a 1.5 km loop trail with wooden boardwalks descends into a humid valley carpeted with moss and ferns. The 9-metre cascade tumbles down a moss-covered rock face, in scenery that resembles a rainforest more than Atlantic Canada.

activities.text: Exploring the ocean floor at low tide on Point Wolfe Beach, preceded by crossing the Point Wolfe Covered Bridge—one of New Brunswick's 58 covered bridges, nicknamed "kissing bridges" because courting couples in carriages would stop inside for privacy.

days_plan.itin_title: Saguenay Fjord & Lac-Saint-Jean Loop
Canadian Maritimes: Halifax to Cape Breton Loop

map_keywords: Hopewell Rocks Flowerpot Rocks New Brunswick

visits.text: La Baie, a Saguenay borough at the head of Ha! Ha! Bay (the name comes from old French 'ha ha,' denoting a dead end or unexpected obstacle—navigators discovered the bay led nowhere). This was once a major timber-shipping port. The historic district preserves buildings from the industrial era and offers views across the bay to the fjord entrance.
Route 170 toward L'Anse-Saint-Jean, hugging the fjord's south shore. The road winds through boreal forest punctuated by glimpses of the fjord's dark waters below.

L'Anse-Saint-Jean: often ranked among Québec's most beautiful villages, this hamlet of about 1,200 is nestled at the head of a cove where the Saint-Jean River flows into the fjord. Its covered bridge, built in 1929, was so iconic it appeared on the old Canadian $1,000 bill. The marina offers spectacular views of the fjord walls rising on either side.

activities.text: Sea kayaking on the fjord from L'Anse-Saint-Jean: paddle at the base of cliffs soaring more than 300 meters above the water, in a fjord that reaches 275 meters deep. Surface water is fresh (it comes from Lac Saint-Jean), while salt water from the Atlantic occupies the depths.

days_plan.itin_title: Saguenay Fjord & Lac-Saint-Jean Loop

places.name: Baddeck

visits.text: Crossing the Strait of Canso via the Canso Causeway, linking Cape Breton to mainland Nova Scotia since 1955. Built with 10 million tonnes of rock, this 1.4 km causeway is the world's deepest (65 m below sea level). Before its construction, Cape Breton Island was accessible only by ferry.

Baddeck, on the shores of Bras d'Or Lake—a vast 1,100 km² saltwater estuary at Cape Breton's heart, designated a UNESCO Biosphere Reserve in 2011. Here Alexander Graham Bell, the telephone's inventor, established his summer estate Beinn Bhreagh in 1886 and lived until his death in 1922. The Alexander Graham Bell National Historic Site chronicles his experiments—not only the telephone, but also the HD-4 hydrofoil that broke the world water speed record in 1919 (114 km/h) on Bras d'Or Lake, and the Silver Dart, the first airplane to fly in Canada (1909).

Fortress of Louisbourg, 35 minutes from Sydney: North America's largest historical reconstruction. Founded by France in 1713 to protect St. Lawrence access and Grand Banks cod fishing, Louisbourg was the most expensive and extensive fortress Europeans built in North America—dubbed the "Gibraltar of the North." The British captured it twice, in 1745 and 1758, then methodically razed it over six years to prevent reuse. In 1961, the Canadian government launched reconstruction of a quarter of the fortified town, employing unemployed coal miners who learned 18th-century masonry techniques. Over one million artifacts were unearthed during excavations.

Return to Baddeck via Cape Breton's east coast, with a possible stop at Louisbourg Lighthouse—the first lighthouse built in Canada (1734). The current lighthouse dates to 1923 and still marks the harbour entrance.

activities.text: Late afternoon walk along Bras d'Or Lake in Baddeck: the village is the traditional starting (or finishing) point of the Cabot Trail.
Historical cuisine tasting at Fortress of Louisbourg: bread from the 18th-century wood-fired bakery, period stews, and cider in reconstructed taverns.

map_keywords: Fortress of Louisbourg National Historic Site

visits.text: Route 362, between Beaupré and Baie-Saint-Paul, winds along the river with panoramas of the Charlevoix mountains and Île d'Orléans. Charlevoix has been a UNESCO World Biosphere Reserve since 1988, owing to its astrobleme—a 56-kilometer-diameter meteorite impact crater 400 million years old that shaped the entire region's geography.

Baie-Saint-Paul, nestled in a valley where the Gouffre River meets the St. Lawrence, is nicknamed the "Artists' Country." Painters like Clarence Gagnon, Marc-Aurèle Fortin, and Jean-Paul Lemieux set up their easels here in the early 20th century. Rue Saint-Jean-Baptiste concentrates Canada's highest density of art galleries. Cirque du Soleil was also born in Baie-Saint-Paul in 1984, emerging from a street performers' troupe led by a fire-breather from Québec City. The Museum of Contemporary Art has organized an international symposium every summer since 1982.

Isle-aux-Coudres, accessible by free ferry from Saint-Joseph-de-la-Rive (15 minutes), is an 11-kilometer-long island in the river. Jacques Cartier named it in 1535 for the abundant hazelnut trees (coudriers) he found. Cycling around the island covers roughly 23 kilometers on flat road. Two heritage-classified mills stand here: a water mill and a windmill, both built in the 19th century, which operated in tandem until 1948.

activities.text: Bicycle tour of Isle-aux-Coudres: 23-kilometer loop on flat road, with stops at heritage mills, artisan shops, and river viewpoints. Bike rentals available near the dock.

days_plan.itin_title: Eastern Canada Road Trip: Toronto, Niagara Falls & Québec City

visits.text: The town of Banff grew around hot springs discovered in November 1883 by three Canadian Pacific Railway workers—William and Tom McCardell and Frank McCabe—who spotted a steaming hole on the slopes of Sulphur Mountain. William descended into the cavity using a ladder hewn from a tree trunk and discovered a pool of hot water beneath stalactites. This discovery led to the creation in 1885 of the Banff Hot Springs Reserve, the nucleus of Canada's first national park.

Ride the Banff Gondola to the summit of Sulphur Mountain at 2,281 metres. The 360-degree panorama embraces the Bow Valley, Mount Rundle, Cascade Mountain, and the snow-clad ranges of the Rockies. A wooden boardwalk leads to the summit of Sanson Peak, where Norman Sanson, a volunteer meteorologist, climbed over 1,000 times between 1903 and 1945 to check his instruments.

Bow Falls, ten minutes on foot from downtown, marks a dramatic drop of the Bow River at the foot of the Fairmont Banff Springs Hotel (built in 1888 by architect Bruce Price in a "château" style inspired by Scottish and French castles). Surprise Corner, an overlook viewpoint, offers Banff's most photographed vista: the falls in the foreground with the château behind.

Lake Minnewanka, 21 km long, is the largest lake in Banff National Park. Its name, in the Stoney language, means "lake of the spirits." A village submerged by rising water during dam construction in 1941 rests several metres beneath the surface.
Tunnel Mountain Drive offers panoramas of the Bow Valley and the hoodoos—columns of sedimentary rock sculpted by erosion, 3 to 7 metres high, capped with more resistant stone blocks.

activities.text: Hike through Johnston Canyon: the trail follows metal catwalks bolted to the canyon wall above Johnston Creek. The Lower Falls are accessible after 1.1 km; the Upper Falls (30 metres high) after 2.7 km. Option to continue to the Ink Pots, five coloured mineral springs in an alpine meadow (5.8 km one way).

Soak at Banff Upper Hot Springs, an outdoor hot spring at 1,585 metres elevation. Water emerges at 47°C and is cooled to about 39°C in the pool. Step through the changing-room doors and into the pool facing Mount Rundle—it's one of the rare places in the world where you bathe in a natural hot spring with views of 3,000-metre peaks.

days_plan.itin_title: Cross-Canada Journey: From Québec City to the Rockies

Western Canada Road Trip: Rockies and Pacific Coast
Western Canada Road Trip: Rockies and Pacific Coast

visits.text: The route between Gaspé and Carleton-sur-Mer follows the Baie des Chaleurs, named by Jacques Cartier in July 1534 because of the summer heat prevailing there. This is where he made the first documented trade exchanges between the French and Mi'kmaq—furs for European objects. This arm of the Gulf of St. Lawrence benefits from a microclimate that makes the water noticeably warmer than the rest of the estuary.

Stop at Bonaventure en route: the Bioparc de la Gaspésie presents the peninsula's five ecosystems—the bay, river, boreal forest, tundra, and shoreline—with indigenous fauna in semi-liberty (black bears, lynx, caribou, otters). The Musée acadien du Québec, on the same street, traces the history of Acadians deported during the Great Upheaval of 1755, some of whom fled to Gaspésie with Mi'kmaq help.

Mont Saint-Joseph rises to 555 meters elevation, just 5 km from the shore. A paved road climbs to the summit, but the hiking trails (30 km total) are more rewarding. At the summit, the view plunges over the Baie des Chaleurs, Carleton's triangular barachois, the Gaspésian backcountry, and the New Brunswick coasts across the bay. Notre-Dame Oratory, a Breton-style chapel built in 1935 by the local community, is notable for its interior mosaics and colored glass windows.

The Carleton-sur-Mer barachois is a brackish body of water separated from the sea by a sand spit—Pointe Tracadigache. This type of coastal lagoon shelters rich biodiversity of birds and fish. The 6-km bike path that loops around it offers a unique perspective on the junction between sea and mountain. As early as the mid-19th century, city dwellers and pilgrims came to enjoy this landscape, making Carleton one of Gaspésie's oldest resort destinations.

Miguasha National Park, inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1999, protects a 380-million-year-old fossil site. In the Mi'kmaq language, 'Miguasha' means 'red land,' referring to the color of the cliffs. The Escuminac Formation, 120 meters thick, contains 21 fossil fish species representing 5 of the 6 major groups that lived in the Devonian—only sharks and rays are missing. The site has yielded more than 18,000 specimens since its discovery in 1842 by geologist Abraham Gesner.

Eusthenopteron foordi, nicknamed the 'Prince of Miguasha,' was a fish equipped with lungs and bony structures in its fins, illustrating the transition from aquatic to terrestrial life. But the museum's current star is Elpistostege watsoni—'Elpi' for short—a 1.6-meter fossil discovered in 2010, unique in the world, considered the closest relative of the first tetrapods. It's a key piece for understanding how vertebrates conquered dry land.

The town of Bonaventure has the world's longest aluminum pedestrian walkway. The Circuit des Bâtisseurs, a bike path lined with illuminated interpretation panels at night, tells the story of the last century in the Baie des Chaleurs.

activities.text: Hike Mont Saint-Joseph: the Taguine trail (5 km) followed by the Cap-Ferré extension (3.6 km) offers an 8.6-km round trip to the summit with progressive views over the Baie des Chaleurs.
Bike tour around the barachois (6 km) late in the day, with a stop at Pointe Tracadigache for sunset and, on some summer evenings, open-air storytelling.

Visit the Miguasha natural history museum and descend to the fossil cliff with a naturalist guide. The 'Through the Geologist's Eye' activity allows you to observe sedimentary layers in situ and understand how fossils form. The 'Evolution of Life' trail (3.5 km) then offers a wooded hike with views over the Restigouche estuary.

Kayak or canoe descent on the Bonaventure River, renowned for its emerald-colored transparent waters. The river, 125 km long, flows through forests and red sandstone cliffs. Guided descents last from 2 to 7 hours depending on the chosen route.

days_plan.itin_title: Gaspésie Loop: From Rimouski to the End of the World
Gaspésie Loop: From Rimouski to the End of the World

visits.text: Green Gables Heritage Place in Cavendish: the green-gabled farmhouse that inspired Lucy Maud Montgomery's novel Anne of Green Gables (1908), translated into 36 languages and selling over 50 million copies. Montgomery, born in Clifton (now New London) in 1874, lived with her grandparents in Cavendish and drew from the island's landscapes—red fields, white sand beaches, birch-lined paths—to create Anne's world. The house, with its east gable chamber and period furnishings, is a literary pilgrimage site, particularly for Japanese visitors.

Prince Edward Island National Park spans 60 km of north coast: red and white sand beaches, red sandstone cliffs sculpted by erosion, dunes covered with marram grass. The red colour of soil and cliffs comes from iron oxide in the 250-million-year-old sandstone. The dunes shelter the piping plover, an endangered species with a few dozen pairs nesting on the island's beaches each summer.

North Rustico fishing harbour, an 18th-century Acadian village: colourful lobster boats line the wharf. Here you can embark on lobster fishing excursions, where a local fisherman shows you how to haul traps, measure catches, and release egg-bearing females—a conservation practice that has preserved Maritime lobster where other stocks have declined.

Points East Coastal Drive scenic route: red-and-white lighthouses punctuate the landscape of potato fields, small wooden churches, and miniature harbours. Stop at Basin Head, whose white sand produces a high-pitched squeak underfoot—a rare phenomenon called "singing sand," caused by the grains' high silica content.

activities.text: Swimming or walking on PEI's north coast beaches: Cavendish Beach with its red sand, Brackley Beach with its dunes, or Greenwich Beach with its rare parabolic dune system. Gulf of St. Lawrence waters warm to 20-22°C in summer.
Coastal drive and viewpoint stops on the island's eastern tip, before heading down to Wood Islands for the Nova Scotia ferry.

days_plan.itin_title: Canadian Maritimes: Halifax to Cape Breton Loop
Canadian Maritimes: Halifax to Cape Breton Loop

places.name: Charlottetown

visits.text: Confederation Bridge: 12.9 km across Northumberland Strait, it's the world's longest bridge over ice-covered waters. Opened May 31, 1997, it links Borden-Carleton (PEI) to Cape Jourimain (NB). Its construction, 1993 to 1997, employed up to 2,500 workers and cost $1.3 billion. The crossing takes 10 minutes at 80 km/h. The bridge is slightly curved—intentionally, to keep drivers alert. Toll is paid only when leaving the island.

Charlottetown, "Birthplace of Confederation": in September 1864, representatives of British North American colonies gathered at Province House—a neoclassical sandstone building erected 1843-1847—to discuss a maritime union. The Canada delegation (Ontario and Quebec) invited themselves, expanding the project into what would become Canada in 1867. The irony: Prince Edward Island, which hosted the founding conference, refused to join Confederation until 1873, pushed by ruinous railway debts.

Stroll Victoria Row, a pedestrian street of colourful Victorian buildings housing restaurants, galleries, and patios. The Confederation Centre of the Arts, opened in 1964 for the Charlottetown Conference centennial, hosts the Anne of Green Gables musical each summer, based on Lucy Maud Montgomery's novel—running continuously for over 60 years.

activities.text: Seafood dinner at Peakes Quay or waterfront restaurants: Malpeque oysters, island mussels, and lobster—PEI alone produces 25% of Canada's potatoes and exports seafood worldwide.

days_plan.itin_title: Canadian Maritimes: Halifax to Cape Breton Loop

map_keywords: Confederation Centre of the Arts Charlottetown

visits.text: Start of the Cabot Trail, a 298 km loop around northern Cape Breton—ranked among the world's most scenic drives. Named for explorer John Cabot who reached Atlantic Canada's shores in 1497 (though historians place his landing in Newfoundland), the road was completed in 1932. Premier Angus MacDonald wanted to reposition Nova Scotia as a tourist destination with Scottish inspiration.

Chéticamp, an Acadian village on Cape Breton's west coast: this francophone community is renowned for its hooked rugs, a textile art passed from mother to daughter since the 19th century. The cultural centre Les Trois Pignons exhibits monumental works, including those of Elizabeth LeFort, dubbed the "Michelangelo of hooking." Saint-Pierre Church, built in 1893, dominates the village with its silver steeple visible from the sea.

Skyline Trail in Cape Breton Highlands National Park (949 km², created 1936): this 8.2 km loop hike crosses boreal forest then emerges onto a cliff promontory overlooking the Gulf of St. Lawrence. Wooden boardwalks lead to viewpoints where you can observe whales below and see the Cabot Trail snaking along mountain flanks. Moose often graze in clearings late in the day. Sunset from the headland is the Cabot Trail's most celebrated moment.

activities.text: Whale-watching excursion departing from Chéticamp or Pleasant Bay: humpback whales, minke whales, and pilot whales frequent Gulf of St. Lawrence waters from June to October.

days_plan.itin_title: Canadian Maritimes: Halifax to Cape Breton Loop

visits.text: Leaving Jasper westward on the Yellowhead Highway (Route 16), Mount Robson appears head-on—at 3,954 metres, it's the highest peak in the Canadian Rockies. A viewpoint and visitor centre at the mountain's foot allow you to contemplate its north face, often veiled in clouds. Only about one in two climbers reaches the summit, such are the capricious weather conditions.

The road continues through Yellowhead Pass (1,131 m) then turns onto Highway 5 south. It passes Blue River and follows the North Thompson River, bordered by forests of spruce and aspen, to Clearwater, gateway to Wells Gray Provincial Park.

Helmcken Falls, in the heart of Wells Gray Provincial Park, plunge 141 metres into a volcanic canyon—Canada's fourth-highest waterfall. Protecting these falls was one of the reasons for creating the park in 1939. Discovered in 1913 by surveyor Robert Lee, they were named in honour of Dr. John Sebastian Helmcken, a Hudson's Bay Company physician who helped bring British Columbia into Confederation in 1871. In winter, spray forms a 50-metre-high ice cone at the base of the falls.

Spahats Creek Falls (75 metres), accessible from a viewpoint just 10 km from the park entrance, tumble into a volcanic gorge with columnar basalt walls. "Spahats" means "bear" in the local First Nations language.

Dawson Falls, nicknamed "Mini Niagara," form a 90-metre-wide curtain of water cascading in steps over 200,000-year-old lava flows. A ten-minute walk through old-growth forest leads to the banks of the Murtle River.

activities.text: Canoe or kayak on Clearwater Lake, 25 km long, formed 6,000 years ago when a lava flow from Kostal Volcano dammed the Clearwater River. The water is remarkably clear—visibility reaches several metres.

days_plan.itin_title: Western Canada Road Trip: Rockies and Pacific Coast
Western Canada Road Trip: Rockies and Pacific Coast

visits.text: The Royal Tyrrell Museum, opened in 1985 in Midland Provincial Park, is Canada's only museum dedicated entirely to paleontology. Named for Joseph Tyrrell, a geologist who discovered the first dinosaur skull in the Red Deer River valley in 1884 while searching for coal. The collection exceeds 160,000 fossil specimens, with over 800 on permanent display. The Borealopelta, an armored nodosaur discovered in the oil sands in 2011, is one of the world's best-preserved fossils—skin and stomach contents included.

Horseshoe Canyon, 17 km west of Drumheller: a spectacular breach carved into the flat prairie, revealing layers of sandstone, mudstone, and coal 70 million years old. The horizontal strata, alternating grey, beige, and ochre, tell the passage from a subtropical climate to the ice age. A short but steep trail descends to the canyon floor.

The Drumheller Hoodoos: these sandstone columns capped with harder blocks result from millions of years of erosion by wind and water. Each pillar stands balanced thanks to its "cap" of hard rock protecting the soft sandstone below. Further along, the Rosedale Suspension Bridge, a wire pedestrian walkway built for coal miners, sways above the valley.

activities.text: Scenic drive on the Dinosaur Trail (48 km loop around Drumheller): cross the Red Deer River on the Bleriot Ferry, one of Alberta's last cable ferries, in service since 1913.

days_plan.itin_title: Cross-Canada Journey: From Québec City to the Rockies

visits.text: Gaspé is the birthplace of French America. On July 24, 1534, Jacques Cartier planted a 30-foot cross here to claim the territory in the name of King François I—a gesture that prompted protests from Iroquoian chief Donnacona. The Musée de la Gaspésie on the boulevard presents the permanent exhibition 'Le Grand Large,' which tells the peninsula's story through 15 sailing vessels, from Viking drakkars to Mi'kmaq ocean-going canoes. In front of the museum, six bronze steles by artist Gérard Leblanc compose the monument commemorating the encounter of two civilizations.

The Christ-Roi Cathedral in Gaspé, consecrated in 1969, is one of the only cathedrals built entirely of wood in North America. Its red cedar structure and modern stained glass windows make it a singular architectural monument. It replaced a previous church destroyed by fire.

The Gespeg Mi'gmaq Culture Interpretation Site recreates a 17th-century Mi'kmaq village, at the time of the first Europeans' arrival. Birchbark wigwams, fire circles, utilitarian objects, and traditional crafts immerse visitors in this millennia-old culture. The word 'Gespeg' means 'land's end' in Mi'kmaq—it gave its name to the town and the entire peninsula.

The Cap-des-Rosiers Lighthouse, erected in 1858, is Canada's tallest lighthouse at 34 meters. It stands at the entrance to Forillon National Park, on a rocky cape battered by gulf waves. Its name comes from explorer Samuel de Champlain, who noted the abundance of wild roses on this point in 1632.

Forillon National Park, created in 1970, spans 245 km² at the northeastern tip of the Gaspésie Peninsula—where the Appalachians end their 3,200-km journey in the St. Lawrence waters. Its creation forced the expropriation of 225 families whose villages were razed, a painful episode in local history. The park's rocks—limestone, sandstone, and mudstone—date from the Ordovician to the Devonian (488 to 359 million years ago). Ten superimposed geological formations in bands are exposed here, a rare phenomenon.

Cap-Bon-Ami offers one of the park's most striking panoramas: vertical limestone cliffs plunge into turquoise water, with a pebble beach at the base. In spring, the Raoul-Roy lookout allows observation of raptor migration. The sector is also the starting point for Mont Saint-Alban, whose observation tower at 285 meters altitude provides 360-degree views—on clear days, you can glimpse Percé Rock and Bonaventure Island in the distance.

The Grande-Grave Heritage Site, on the park's southern coast, preserves buildings from the cod fishing era. The Hyman & Sons general store, built in the 1860s, functioned as a trading post where fishermen exchanged their catch for goods. Anse-Blanchette recreates the life of a farming-fishing family from the early 20th century. Fort Péninsule, near Penouille, was fortified during World War II to defend Gaspé Bay against German submarines.

The Les Graves trail leads to Cap-Gaspé, where you'll find 'the end of the world': a red and white lighthouse and a lookout perched above the sea. This is where the Gaspésie Peninsula ends in a rocky point plunging into gulf waters. Whales are regularly observed offshore from this point.

activities.text: Walk the Jacques-Cartier Promenade along the York River and Gaspé Bay. Period houses and shops line the route, and on some summer evenings, a projection show recounts Cartier's arrival on a giant screen mimicking his ship's sails.
Mackerel fishing from the Gaspé wharf: the activity is open to all, with or without experience, and equipment is easy to find on-site.
Hike Mont Saint-Alban: 7.8-km loop starting from Cap-Bon-Ami. The climb is steep for the first kilometer, then the trail crosses boreal forest before reaching the observation tower at 285 meters.
Hike to Cap-Gaspé via the Les Graves trail: 8-km round trip from Anse-aux-Amérindiens, following the Gaspé Bay coast between forest and shoreline.
Sunset sea kayaking in Gaspé Bay, departing from Cap-aux-Os beach. The 3-hour guided outings allow observation of grey seals, seabirds, and the sun descending behind Forillon's cliffs.

days_plan.itin_title: Gaspésie Loop: From Rimouski to the End of the World
Gaspésie Loop: From Rimouski to the End of the World

places.name: Halifax

visits.text: Halifax Citadel, perched on the hill overlooking the harbour: this is where Edward Cornwallis landed in 1749 with 2,576 British settlers to establish Halifax as a strategic counterweight to the French fortress at Louisbourg. The current star-shaped fort is the fourth built on this site—completed in 1856 after nearly 30 years of construction. Its star shape enabled covering all firing angles. The irony: the introduction of rifled cannons shortly after its completion rendered its walls obsolete. It was never attacked.

Descend to the Halifax Waterfront Boardwalk, one of the longest wooden boardwalks in North America. The Maritime Museum of the Atlantic, on the waterfront, houses the largest collection of Titanic artifacts outside Belfast—Halifax was the closest port to the 1912 sinking and dispatched rescue ships. The museum also chronicles the Halifax Explosion of December 6, 1917: the collision of two ships in the harbour triggered the largest human-made explosion before Hiroshima, killing nearly 2,000 people and obliterating the city's north end.

Halifax Public Gardens, opened in 1867, are the oldest surviving Victorian public gardens in North America. Across 16 acres, the winding paths, wrought-iron bandstand, and flower beds have remained virtually unchanged since the Victorian era. A 2003 hurricane felled hundreds of century-old trees, but the garden was meticulously restored.

Return route to Halifax via the Trans-Canada. Possible stop in Truro, where the Salmon River offers a tidal bore visible from Tidal Bore Viewing Park. The Bay of Fundy narrows here into the Minas Basin, where tides reach their 16-metre maximum—the world record. Twice daily, 100 billion tonnes of water flow in and out of the bay—more than the combined flow of all Earth's rivers.

Return to Halifax: the Seaport Farmers' Market, operating since 1750, is North America's oldest continuously operating farmers' market. Set in a modern waterfront building, it gathers local producers of cheese, smoked seafood, sourdough bread, and craft cider.

activities.text: Evening in the historic quarter's pubs: Halifax has a reputation for having more bars per capita than any other Canadian city. Alexander Keith's Brewery, founded in 1820, is one of Canada's oldest operating breweries.
Final evening in Halifax: sunset stroll along the waterfront from Bishop's Landing Wharf to the historic harbour.

days_plan.itin_title: Canadian Maritimes: Halifax to Cape Breton Loop

map_keywords: Maritime Museum of the Atlantic Halifax

visits.text: Continue the Cabot Trail's north slope: the road runs from Chéticamp to Pleasant Bay, hugs the rugged Meat Cove coastline (Nova Scotia's northernmost inhabited village), then descends toward the east slope through steep valleys and Gaelic hamlets. Road signs are bilingual English-Gaelic in several villages, testimony to Scottish Highland settlers driven by 19th-century Clearances.

Ingonish, on the national park's east coast: Ingonish Beach—one of Canada's rare beaches where fresh and salt water meet—is naturally divided by a sand barrier. On one side, a freshwater lake; on the other, the Atlantic Ocean. The Middle Head Trail (2 km return, easy) ventures onto a rocky peninsula between two bays, offering cliff and sea views.

activities.text: Hike the Franey Trail (7.4 km loop): steep climb through Acadian forest to a 425 m summit with panoramic views of the Atlantic, cliffs, and Clyburn Valley. One of the Cabot Trail's finest viewpoints.

days_plan.itin_title: Canadian Maritimes: Halifax to Cape Breton Loop

places.name: Jacques Cartier

visits.text: Route 175 North from Québec City: after 30 minutes, you enter the Laurentides Wildlife Reserve, a vast boreal forest stretching 200 km between Québec and the Saguenay. No villages, very few gas stations along this stretch—fill up before you leave. The four-lane highway cuts through a landscape of lakes, black spruce, and rounded hills sculpted by glaciers.

Jacques-Cartier National Park (670 km², established 1981): just 30 minutes from Québec City, the park protects one of Québec's most dramatic glacial valleys. The Jacques-Cartier Valley drops more than 550 meters—the walls plunge from a high mountain plateau down to a meandering river at the bottom. Up top, boreal conifer forest; in the valley floor, deciduous trees (sugar maples, yellow birch) thrive in a sheltered microclimate.

Hike the Loups Trail (11 km, 447 m elevation gain, difficult): the park's most spectacular viewpoint, with a bird's-eye view down to the Jacques-Cartier Valley and river far below. Shorter alternative: the Éperon Trail (5.4 km, 250 m gain, moderate) also delivers stunning views over the valley and the river at l'Épaule.

activities.text: Canoe or kayak descent on the Jacques-Cartier River: the river alternates between calm stretches and rapids. The park offers boat rentals and a shuttle service for downstream runs (up to 26 km). A unique way to experience the valley from below, framed by forested walls rising on both sides.

days_plan.itin_title: Saguenay Fjord & Lac-Saint-Jean Loop

map_keywords: Parc national de la Jacques-Cartier Québec

places.name: Jasper

visits.text: The Icefields Parkway, 230 km between Lake Louise and Jasper, traverses the heart of the Rockies. Stop at Bow Lake, a turquoise lake at the foot of Crowfoot Glacier, then climb to Bow Summit viewpoint (2,069 m, the highest point on the highway) to gaze upon Peyto Lake, recognizable by its fox shape and intense blue.
Stop at Mistaya Canyon, a narrow gorge where the Mistaya River plunges into a limestone defile polished by water. The footbridge above the canyon lets you see the potholes carved by whirlpools.

The Columbia Icefield, 325 km² in area and up to 365 metres thick, is the largest icefield in the Rockies. Discovered in 1898 by explorer J. Norman Collie from the summit of Mount Athabasca, it feeds three oceans (Pacific, Atlantic, and Arctic) through its meltwater—a unique case of triple continental divide. The Athabasca Glacier, one of its six main glaciers, has retreated 1.5 km in 125 years.

Athabasca Falls, 30 km south of Jasper, are not the highest (23 metres) but rank among the most powerful in the Rockies. The Athabasca River plunges into a quartzite gorge it has carved since the end of the last glaciation. Marked trails and viewing platforms allow you to observe the water's power from several angles.

Maligne Canyon, a 50-metre-deep gorge cut through limestone by the Maligne River, is explored via a network of six numbered bridges. Between bridges 1 and 2, the walls are separated by only a few metres while the river roars below.

Pyramid Lake and Pyramid Island, connected to shore by a small wooden bridge, form one of Jasper's most peaceful landscapes. The glacial lake reflects Pyramid Mountain (2,763 m), from which it takes its name.

activities.text: Jasper SkyTram for panoramas of the Rockies
Hike the Bald Hills trail and watch for elk and black bears

Cruise on Maligne Lake to Spirit Island. Twenty-two kilometres long, Maligne is the largest natural lake in the Canadian Rockies. Spirit Island, a tiny wooded islet surrounded by turquoise water and snowy peaks, became one of Canada's most reproduced images after appearing in a calendar series in the 1950s. The cruise lasts about 90 minutes round trip.
Hike the Valley of the Five Lakes, an easy loop traversing five small lakes in shades from jade to deep blue, each coloured differently according to its rocky bottom composition and light.

Ride the Jasper SkyTram to the summit of Whistlers Mountain at 2,277 metres. It's Canada's longest and highest aerial tramway. From the summit, on clear days, the view extends to Mount Robson (3,954 m, highest peak in the Canadian Rockies), the Athabasca Valley, and the mountain ranges beyond.

Western Canada Road Trip: Rockies and Pacific Coast
Western Canada Road Trip: Rockies and Pacific Coast
Western Canada Road Trip: Rockies and Pacific Coast

map_keywords: Valley of the Five Lakes Jasper

visits.text: Stop in Hope, a small town at the foot of the Cascades where the Fraser Valley narrows. Six kilometres from downtown, the Othello Tunnels (Coquihalla Canyon Provincial Park) are five railway tunnels carved through granite between 1911 and 1916 for the Kettle Valley Railway. Abandoned since 1959, the pedestrian path traverses the tunnels and crosses the narrow Coquihalla River gorge on old railway bridges—a spectacular passage between vertical rock walls.

The route then takes the Coquihalla Highway (Route 5), built in 1986, which crosses a pass at 1,244 metres, before turning onto Route 97C through the high plateaus. Arrival in Kelowna reveals Okanagan Lake, 135 km long and 232 metres deep. According to the oral tradition of the Syilx First Nations, the lake harbours N'ha-a-itk (Ogopogo), a lake serpent.

Stroll through Kelowna's Waterfront Park and City Park, landscaped along the lake's eastern shore. City Park features a large sandy beach and an unobstructed view of the William R. Bennett floating bridge (694 metres, opened 2008) linking Kelowna to West Kelowna.

Knox Mountain Park overlooks Kelowna from its northern shore. The trail climbs 2 km to the lookout where the panorama embraces Okanagan Lake, the terraced orchards, and semi-arid hills carpeted with sagebrush—the valley receives less than 400 mm of rain annually.
Hot Sands Beach, on the eastern shore, is Kelowna's most popular beach. The lake water, of glacial origin, warms sufficiently in summer for swimming thanks to the shallow depth near shore.

activities.text: Hike in Knox Mountain Park for views over Okanagan Lake

Wine tasting in the Okanagan Valley, British Columbia's largest wine region with over 200 estates across 250 km. Viticulture dates to the 1850s when missionaries planted vines for sacramental wine. After near-disappearance during Prohibition, revival came only in the 1930s with Calona Vineyards, the province's first commercial winery. Noble grape varieties (Pinot Noir, Chardonnay, Riesling) replaced hybrids in the 1970s-80s. The continental microclimate—14 hours of daily sunshine in summer—produces award-winning wines, notably icewines.

days_plan.itin_title: Western Canada Road Trip: Rockies and Pacific Coast
Western Canada Road Trip: Rockies and Pacific Coast

visits.text: Kenora, Ontario's last city before Manitoba, sits on the shores of Lake of the Woods—a sprawling lake covering 4,350 km² dotted with over 14,500 islands. A long day's drive through boreal forest, with stops at lookouts along the Trans-Canada Highway. The region was a crossroads of fur trade routes between the Great Lakes and the Prairies.
Wander the lakefront in downtown Kenora and spot Husky the Muskie, a 12-meter-tall giant muskellunge statue—a nod to the sport fishing that made Lake of the Woods famous.

activities.text: Sunset on Lake of the Woods: the calm waters mirror the pines and rocks of the Canadian Shield, a landscape typical of northwestern Ontario.

days_plan.itin_title: Cross-Canada Journey: From Québec City to the Rockies

visits.text: Hautes-Gorges-de-la-Rivière-Malbaie National Park protects deep valleys whose cliffs rank among the tallest east of the Canadian Rockies. The Malbaie River carved these spectacular gorges over millions of years. The name "Acropole des Draveurs" comes from a 1972 poem by Félix-Antoine Savard honoring the log drivers—workers who, each spring at snowmelt, cast felled logs into the river to float them downstream to mills.

activities.text: Acropole des Draveurs hike: 10.8 kilometers round-trip, 800 meters of elevation gain, summit at 1,048 meters. The first two kilometers climb steeply on natural stone steps. Beyond, the maple forest gives way to alpine tundra. Three successive summits offer plunging views over the Malbaie River 800 meters below and the Charlevoix mountain ranges. At the third summit, view two glacial lakes. Bring warm clothing: it's 5 to 10°C cooler at the wind-exposed summit.

Alternative for those not attempting the big hike: rabaska (large canoe) or tour boat cruise on the Malbaie River at the gorge bottom. Upward view of vertiginous forest-covered walls. The Riverain trail (easy, 4 km) follows the river with cliff viewpoints.

days_plan.itin_title: Eastern Canada Road Trip: Toronto, Niagara Falls & Québec City

visits.text: OPTION A - Return via Route 155 (Saint-Maurice Valley): this spectacular 230-km road follows the Saint-Maurice River through nearly pristine boreal forest. The Saint-Maurice River, once the highway for log drivers floating timber down to mills in Trois-Rivières, flows 563 km from the Gouin Reservoir. The road follows its meanders, rapids, and lake-like widenings. The route's most striking moment: when you leave Lac Saint-Jean from Chambord and the immense expanse of water appears suddenly, 'like the sea.'

Stop in La Tuque, a small forestry town on the Saint-Maurice—a rest and refueling point midway. The town was an important log-driving center and remains shaped by the forest industry. Félix-Antoine Savard, author of the novel Menaud, Master Logger (1937) celebrating Saint-Maurice log drivers, drew on this region for his work.

OPTION B - Return via Chicoutimi → Tadoussac (Route 172 East): this alternative follows the Saguenay Fjord's north shore to its mouth. Stop at the Sainte-Marguerite River, where belugas regularly visit the shallow cove waters (observable from a platform on shore). Arrival in Tadoussac, at the confluence of the Saguenay and St. Lawrence, is one of Québec's most beautiful landscapes. This option is recommended if you haven't done the Eastern Canada road trip and want to discover Tadoussac.

activities.text: If option A: stop at La Mauricie National Park (536 km², midway between La Tuque and Trois-Rivières) if time allows—maple forest, lakes, and waterfalls in the Laurentian hills.

days_plan.itin_title: Saguenay Fjord & Lac-Saint-Jean Loop

places.name: Lake Louise

visits.text: Drive via Golden and Kicking Horse Pass to Banff National Park. Lake Louise, at 1,731 metres elevation, stretches 2.4 km long and reaches 70 metres deep. Its turquoise colour comes from rock flour—particles ground by the Victoria Glacier. The Stoney-Nakoda called it Ho-run-num-nay, "the lake of little fishes." In 1882, Thomas Wilson, a guide and packer working for the Canadian Pacific, became the first non-Indigenous person to see it.

The Fairmont Château Lake Louise, whose construction dates to 1890, occupies the northern shore facing the Victoria Glacier. The lakeshore trail (3.4 km, flat) follows the shore to the western end. The lake, named in honour of Princess Louise Caroline Alberta, daughter of Queen Victoria, gave its name to the province.

Moraine Lake, 14 km by road, occupies the Valley of the Ten Peaks—an amphitheatre of summits rising above 3,000 metres. Its cobalt blue colour graced the reverse of the old Canadian twenty-dollar bill. Road accessible June to October only.

activities.text: Plain of Six Glaciers hike: the trail starts from the end of Lake Louise and climbs through a moraine to a high-altitude teahouse. Close-up views of the Victoria, Lefroy, and Aberdeen glaciers.
Alternative: hike to Lake Agnes Tea House, a teahouse at 2,135 metres elevation beside a small alpine lake. The trail passes through conifer forest and by Mirror Lake.

days_plan.itin_title: Western Canada Road Trip: Rockies and Pacific Coast
Western Canada Road Trip: Rockies and Pacific Coast

map_keywords: Plain of Six Glaciers Lake Louise

visits.text: Bic National Park, established in 1984, covers 33.2 km² between Route 132 and the St. Lawrence estuary. This territory has been inhabited for over 8,000 years, as evidenced by some thirty Indigenous archaeological sites. Samuel de Champlain anchored here in 1603 and gave his name to the peak dominating the park. The landscape consists of rock ridges folded by the Appalachians, forming an intricate lace of bays, coves, headlands and islands along 30 km of coastline.

Pic Champlain rises to 346 meters elevation—the highest point in the natural region of the estuary's southern shore. The trail climbs through a maple grove before opening onto a panorama embracing the islands, Ha! Ha! Bay, and the 34-km-wide estuary. The park harbors the continent's highest concentration of North American porcupines and harbor seal colonies observable from Anse aux Bouleaux Ouest.

Le Grand Rassemblement in Sainte-Flavie marks the 'gateway to Gaspésie.' In 1986, sculptor Marcel Gagnon began placing life-sized concrete statues in the St. Lawrence waters. Today, about 120 figures emerge from the river with the tides, in a long silent procession toward land. The artwork transforms with light and water level—at low tide, the silhouettes rise in full; at high tide, only heads protrude.

The Reford Gardens in Grand-Métis have been a National Historic Site of Canada since 1995. Elsie Reford, a self-taught horticulturist who didn't take up gardening until age 54 following an appendix operation, transformed between 1926 and 1958 a fishing camp inherited from her uncle George Stephen (founder of Canadian Pacific Railway) into English-style gardens. The 18-hectare estate shelters 3,000 plant species and varieties, including the famous Himalayan blue poppy. The microclimate created by the confluence of the Mitis River and the St. Lawrence allows cultivation of species found nowhere else in Canada. Villa Estevan, built in 1887, is a historic 37-room house.

activities.text: Hike the Grand Tour at Bic Park: this 8.7-km loop follows the rocky shoreline and is only passable at low tide. Start about two hours before the lowest tide to explore the rock formations and observe seals on the rocks.

Sea kayaking excursion through the Bic archipelago, weaving between islands and headlands. Harbor seal colonies bask on the rocks and common eider ducks nest on the islets. In the background, the setting sun illuminates the cliffs.

days_plan.itin_title: Gaspésie Loop: From Rimouski to the End of the World

places.name: Lunenburg

visits.text: Peggy's Cove Lighthouse (Peggy's Point Lighthouse), 43 km from Halifax: perched atop a massive granite outcrop battered by the Atlantic, this is Canada's most photographed lighthouse. The current octagonal reinforced-concrete structure dates to 1914, replacing a wooden lighthouse built in 1868. Peggy's Cove village, founded in 1811 by six German families, has only about thirty permanent residents. The name likely derives from Margaret ("Peggy"), nickname for nearby Sainte-Marguerite Bay—or, according to local legend, from a shipwreck survivor named Margaret rescued by fishermen.

One kilometre northwest of the lighthouse, the Swissair Flight 111 Memorial commemorates the tragedy of September 2, 1998, when an aircraft crashed offshore with 229 people aboard. Two granite monuments—one at Bayswater Beach (landward), the other here—face toward the crash site.

Old Town Lunenburg, designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1995: founded in 1753, it's the best-preserved planned Protestant settlement in North America. The initial 1,453 settlers—primarily German speakers from Hanover, Switzerland, and Montbéliard—were installed according to a grid plan drawn in London, with streets at right angles regardless of topography. The result: vertiginously steep streets. About 400 significant buildings survive, 70% dating from the 18th and 19th centuries, nearly all brightly painted wood.

Bluenose II, moored at Lunenburg's wharf in summer, is a faithful replica of the Bluenose, Canada's most celebrated fishing schooner. Launched in 1921 at Lunenburg's Smith and Rhuland shipyard, the original Bluenose remained undefeated for 17 years in international schooner races. Its image has graced the Canadian ten-cent coin since 1937. The original schooner was sold as a freighter in 1942 and sank on a reef off Haiti in 1946. Bluenose II, built in 1963 by the same workers from the same plans, was gifted to the province for a symbolic dollar.

activities.text: Seafood dinner on Lunenburg's waterfront: lobster, scallops, and clam chowder in the old fishing port's converted warehouses.

days_plan.itin_title: Canadian Maritimes: Halifax to Cape Breton Loop

map_keywords: Fisheries Museum of the Atlantic Lunenburg

places.name: Mauricie

visits.text: La Mauricie National Park, created in 1970, protects 536 km² of Canadian Shield—rock formations among the continent's oldest, formed a billion years ago. Glaciers sculpted the rounded hills and carved over 150 lakes connected by portages. The Promenade Road crosses the park for 63 kilometers from entrance to entrance, through mixed forest of sugar maples, yellow birches, and balsam firs. Indigenous rock paintings were discovered on a cliff at Lac Wapizagonke, 17 kilometers long.

activities.text: Lac-Solitaire trail hike: gradual ascent through mature maple forest, punctuated by lookouts with plunging views over surrounding lakes. At the summit, a solar-heated warming cabin offers shelter. The park shelters 50 mammal species (eastern wolf, black bear, moose, beaver) and 180 bird species, including the common loon, the park's emblem.

Canoeing on one of the park's 150 lakes. Rentals available at Shewenegan, Wapizagonke, or Lac-Édouard picnic areas. Before 1970, this territory was exploited by logging companies and private hunting and fishing clubs reserved for an anglophone elite. The park ended these privileges to open nature to all.

days_plan.itin_title: Eastern Canada Road Trip: Toronto, Niagara Falls & Québec City

map_keywords: Parc national de la Mauricie Québec

visits.text: Petitcodiac River tidal bore in Moncton (Bore Park): twice daily, the rising Bay of Fundy tide surges upriver, creating a wave advancing against the current. Before construction of a causeway in 1968, the Petitcodiac bore reached 2 metres high—among the world's largest. Progressive silting of the river nearly killed the phenomenon, but partial causeway demolition in 2010 and a new bridge opening in 2023 allowed the bore to regain vigour.

Magnetic Hill, a natural curiosity on Moncton's outskirts: at the bottom of a slope, put your vehicle in neutral—it appears to roll uphill by itself. The optical illusion, caused by the surrounding landscape distorting perception of horizontal, has drawn the curious since the 1930s.

Monument Lefebvre in Memramcook, 20 km south of Moncton: this 1897 building, a National Historic Site, houses the first Acadian college founded by Father Camille Lefebvre in 1864—precisely when the Charlottetown Conference was convening. Here the Acadian cultural renaissance took root after a century of post-deportation silence. The first Acadian National Convention met here in 1881, where delegates chose August 15 as the Acadian national holiday and the tricolour with star as their flag.

activities.text: Moncton, New Brunswick's only officially bilingual city: stroll downtown among francophone and anglophone shops. The city sits at the junction of Acadia and English Canada—a third of the population is francophone.

days_plan.itin_title: Canadian Maritimes: Halifax to Cape Breton Loop

places.name: Mont Tremblant

visits.text: Tremblant's pedestrian village, at the mountain's base, is an ensemble of colorful buildings inspired by traditional Québec villages, constructed from 1991 onward. Access via a free gondola from the parking area. Pedestrian lanes climb in tiers to the ski base, with shops, restaurants, and terraced patios.

activities.text: Hiking in Mont-Tremblant National Park, Québec's oldest and largest park (1,510 km²), created in 1895—only 23 years after Yellowstone. The Algonquins called it "Manitonga Soutana," the Mountain of Spirits, perceiving rumbling sounds from within. The La Roche and La Corniche trail (8.7 km loop) offers panoramas over the glacial valley of Lac Monroe and the Mont-Tremblant massif, which peaks at 875 meters. The park contains 400 lakes, 6 rivers, and 40 mammal species, including wolves.

Via ferrata du Diable: secured route on the rock face of Vache Noire, at the entrance to the Diable sector. Beams, monkey bridges, and suspended walkways above the Diable River, 200 meters up. Groups of 8 maximum, guided.

days_plan.itin_title: Eastern Canada Road Trip: Toronto, Niagara Falls & Québec City

map_keywords: Parc national du Mont-Tremblant secteur Diable

visits.text: Place d'Armes is Montréal's historic heart. At its center, the statue of Maisonneuve, the city's founder in 1642, faces Notre-Dame Basilica. Surrounding the square: the Old Seminary of Saint-Sulpice (1685), the oldest building still standing in Montréal, and the Bank of Montréal tower (1847), Canada's first bank.

Notre-Dame Basilica, built from 1824 to 1829 by Irish-born architect James O'Donnell from New York, is Canada's first Gothic Revival church. It could accommodate up to 10,000 worshippers—for 50 years it was the largest church in North America, all denominations included. The interior, renovated from 1872 to 1880 by architect Victor Bourgeau, is a profusion of carved wood, gilding, and deep blue. The great bell "Jean-Baptiste," installed in 1848, weighs 10,900 kilograms. O'Donnell, born Protestant, converted to Catholicism before his death in 1830; he rests in the crypt of the church he designed.

The Old Port extends 2.5 kilometers along the St. Lawrence River. A former industrial port zone fallen into disuse, it was redeveloped between 1983 and 1992 as a public promenade. Converted warehouses house the Montréal Science Centre. Offshore on Île Sainte-Hélène stands the Biosphere—the former U.S. pavilion from Expo 67, a geodesic dome designed by Buckminster Fuller.

Rue Saint-Paul, Montréal's oldest street, runs through Old Montréal between Place Royale and Bonsecours Market. The latter, built in 1847 in neoclassical style with its silvered dome, served as public market, city hall, and even parliament of the United Province of Canada. Cobblestones, cast-iron lampposts, and grey stone facades give the quarter a European atmosphere.

Mount Royal Park, designed by Frederick Law Olmsted (the same landscape architect as New York's Central Park), opened in 1876. The Olmsted Road, the park's main trail, winds gently upward through 200 hectares of urban forest to the Kondiaronk Belvedere, named for the Wendat chief who signed the Great Peace of Montréal with the French in 1701. The view sweeps across downtown, the St. Lawrence River, and on clear days, the Adirondack Mountains to the south. The Mount Royal Chalet, in Beaux-Arts style, displays paintings by Québec artists like Marc-Aurèle Fortin and Paul-Émile Borduas. The summit cross, standing 31 meters tall, was erected in 1924.

The Plateau Mont-Royal is recognizable by its exterior spiral staircases, characteristic of Montréal housing built between 1880 and 1930—a solution to maximize interior space on narrow lots. Avenue Mont-Royal, the neighborhood's commercial artery, alternates independent cafés, used bookstores, and sidewalk terraces. The quarter was home to francophone artists and writers for decades.

Mile End is Montréal's creative quarter, wedged between Boulevard Saint-Laurent and Avenue du Parc. Its former textile workshops have been converted into artist studios and tech startups. Here unfolds the rivalry between Montréal's two bagel bakeries, wood-firing around the clock since the 1950s—bagels smaller, denser, and sweeter than New York's, hand-rolled and poached in honey water. The neighborhood's walls wear over 300 murals—Montréal counts among the world's street art capitals.

activities.text: Hockey game at the Bell Centre
Final dinner in the heart of Old Montréal
Wood-fired bagel tasting in Mile End, followed by coffee at one of the neighborhood's independent roasters.

days_plan.itin_title: Cross-Canada Journey: From Québec City to the Rockies
Cross-Canada Journey: From Québec City to the Rockies
Eastern Canada Road Trip: Toronto, Niagara Falls & Québec City
Eastern Canada Road Trip: Toronto, Niagara Falls & Québec City

visits.text: Niagara Falls on the Canadian side delivers the most dramatic view of Horseshoe Falls: 57 meters high, roughly 670 meters across the crest line. About 2,800 cubic meters of water per second are permitted to flow over the falls during daytime in tourist season—at night and off-season, up to 75% of the flow is diverted to hydroelectric plants. Approximately 90% of the visible flow passes over Horseshoe Falls. Erosion pushes the falls upstream about 30 centimeters annually. 12,500 years ago, they stood 11 kilometers downstream at Queenston.

Journey Behind the Falls: tunnels carved through bedrock behind the water curtain lead to two observation decks one-third of the way down Horseshoe Falls. You feel the vibration of the water mass and permanent mist. A yellow raincoat is provided—it will be soaked through.

Niagara-on-the-Lake, 25 kilometers north of the falls, is a former Loyalist town founded in 1781 by refugees from the American Revolution. Named Newark, it served as the first capital of Upper Canada from 1792 to 1796. Razed by Americans during the War of 1812, it was rebuilt in remarkably preserved Georgian and Victorian style. Its historic core, anchored by Queen Street with old-fashioned shops, has been a National Historic Site since 2004.

activities.text: Boat cruise to the base of the falls: vessels approach within meters of Horseshoe Falls, enveloped in permanent spray and deafening thunder. Duration approximately 20 minutes on the water. Poncho provided.

Ice wine tasting at Niagara Peninsula vineyards. The region counts over 70 wineries. Ice wine is crafted from grapes harvested frozen on the vine at temperatures of -8°C or colder. The peninsula benefits from a microclimate tempered by Lakes Ontario and Erie.

days_plan.itin_title: Eastern Canada Road Trip: Toronto, Niagara Falls & Québec City

visits.text: North Bay, "Gateway to the North," developed with the arrival of the Canadian Pacific Railway in 1882. The restored historic station now houses a museum tracing the region's railway history. The city also owes its fame to the Dionne quintuplets, born here in 1934—an event that captured worldwide attention.

Lake Nipissing, Ontario's third-largest lake entirely within the province (875 km², 65 km long), was a major trade route for the Anishinaabe peoples. Its Ojibwe name paradoxically means "little body of water"—compared to the nearby Great Lakes. In 1610, explorer Étienne Brûlé became the first European to navigate these waters, along the fur trade route linking the Ottawa River to Lake Huron via the French River.

activities.text: Sunset walk along North Bay's waterfront: the white sand beaches of Shabogesic Beach rank among Ontario's finest, facing the lake's shallow waters (average depth only 4.5 m).

days_plan.itin_title: Cross-Canada Journey: From Québec City to the Rockies

places.name: Ottawa

visits.text: Parliament Hill overlooks the Ottawa River from a limestone outcrop. The Gothic Revival buildings, constructed between 1859 and 1866, were ravaged by fire in February 1916—only the Library, saved by an employee who closed its iron doors, survived. The Peace Tower, completed in 1927, rises 92 meters. Its carillon of 53 bells, commissioned to commemorate the 1918 armistice, still rings daily over the capital.

The Rideau Canal stretches 202 km from Ottawa to Lake Ontario. Built between 1826 and 1832 under the direction of Colonel John By, it mobilized up to 5,000 workers annually—mainly Irish immigrants and French Canadians—hundreds of whom died from malaria or accidents. Designed as a military route after the War of 1812, it became a commercial waterway and later a natural skating rink in winter. A UNESCO site since 2007, it's the only World Heritage Site in Ontario.

ByWard Market, operating since 1826, bears Colonel By's name. It's one of the oldest and largest public markets in Canada. Stalls of local products—cheeses, maple syrup, pastries—sit alongside art galleries and restaurants in 19th-century stone and brick buildings.

Cross the bridge over the canal to the National Gallery of Canada, recognizable by its granite and glass towers. It houses the world's largest collection of Inuit art—stone, bone, and ivory sculptures from communities across the Far North. The building, designed by architect Moshe Safdie and opened in 1988, plays with natural light through glass canopies that frame views of Parliament Hill.

activities.text: Bike ride or walk along the Rideau Canal in late afternoon: the path follows the Ottawa Locks, the eight-step flight of locks connecting the canal to the Ottawa River.

days_plan.itin_title: Cross-Canada Journey: From Québec City to the Rockies
Eastern Canada Road Trip: Toronto, Niagara Falls & Québec City

map_keywords: Musée des beaux-arts du Canada Ottawa

places.name: Perce

visits.text: Percé Rock is a limestone monolith 433 meters long and 88 meters high, pierced by a natural arch 15 meters wide. The vertical strata composing it belong to the Forillon Formation and date from the Lower Devonian, between 394 and 418 million years ago. They contain fossils of brachiopods and trilobites. Until 1845, the rock had two arches—the second collapsed. Marine erosion drops several tons of rock each year, and access to the base of the rock is prohibited for safety reasons.

The Percé UNESCO Global Geopark offers 18 km of hiking trails through 500-million-year-old geology. The Mont Sainte-Anne trail leads to a natural cave, a spectacular crevasse, and a forest of cedars twisted by wind, nicknamed the 'magic forest.' At the top of the climb, a glass platform suspended at 200 meters altitude overlooks the village and Percé Rock—Gaspésie's most photographed spot. The immersive Tektonik exhibition in the welcome pavilion takes you back through Percé's geological time.

The Promenade de la Grave runs along the village waterfront. Percé takes its name from Basque and Breton fishermen who frequented the bay as early as the 16th century for cod. The village was an important fishing center under the regime of Jersey companies, including Charles Robin & Company, which dominated the dried cod trade for over a century.

Bonaventure Island and Percé Rock National Park protects exceptional natural, historical, and geological heritage. Bonaventure Island, covering 4.16 km², harbors the world's most accessible Northern Gannet colony—about 110,000 birds nest each summer on the red conglomerate cliffs of the eastern coast. The Northern Gannet is a seabird with white plumage, an orange-tinted head, and black wings, with a wingspan reaching up to 2 meters. It dives like an arrow at over 100 km/h to catch fish.

The island was inhabited by fishing families until 1971. The historic village, with its houses and vigneaux (wooden structures for drying cod), testifies to a vanished way of life. Le Boutillier House, the former fishing manager's residence, is open to visitors free of charge and displays period furniture.

The Emerald River Falls, in Percé's backcountry, lives up to its name: water cascades into a natural basin of intense turquoise. A short forest trail leads there for a refreshing break away from the coast.

activities.text: Hike the Geopark trails: the Mont Sainte-Anne loop (about 4 km) passes through the crevasse, cave, and magic forest before reaching the suspended glass platform.
Sunset walk along La Grave and Cap Mont-Joli for the best views of Percé Rock bathed in golden light.

Cruise to Bonaventure Island: the boat first circles close to Percé Rock, then runs along the island's cliffs where thousands of birds nest—black-legged kittiwakes, cormorants, razorbills, and Atlantic puffins. Once landed on the island, take the Sentier des Mousses (3.1 km) going and the Chemin du Roy returning for varied landscapes. At the end, the Northern Gannet colony extends just meters from the observation boardwalk.
Whale watching cruise: seven whale species frequent the waters off Percé, including minke whales, fin whales, and occasionally humpback whales.

days_plan.itin_title: Gaspésie Loop: From Rimouski to the End of the World
Gaspésie Loop: From Rimouski to the End of the World

map_keywords: Colonie fous de Bassan île Bonaventure

visits.text: Dolbeau-Mistassini, which bills itself as the 'wild blueberry capital of the world.' Every August, the Blueberry Festival celebrates this small fruit emblematic of the region. Blueberry fields—endless expanses where wild lowbush blueberries (Vaccinium angustifolium) grow—cover thousands of hectares around the lake. In season (July-August), you can pick your own in fields open to the public.

Péribonka: this village on Lac Saint-Jean's north shore houses the museum dedicated to Louis Hémon's novel Maria Chapdelaine. Hémon was a Breton writer who arrived in Canada in 1911 and settled in Péribonka in 1912 as a farmhand with the Bédard family. In a few months he wrote a novel about the lives of pioneer settlers clearing land around Lac-Saint-Jean—the cold, the isolation, the attachment to the land. Hémon left Péribonka in June 1913 and died tragically on July 8, struck by a train in Ontario, at age 33. The novel, published posthumously in 1914, was translated into more than 25 languages and became one of the most famous texts about rural Québec.

Parc régional des Grandes-Rivières du lac Saint-Jean: located at the confluence of the Mistassini and Péribonka rivers, two of the most powerful rivers feeding the lake. The park offers hiking trails along the riverbanks, kayaking, and observation points overlooking the rapids. The Péribonka River, made famous by Hémon's novel, flows 451 km from deep in the boreal forest.

activities.text: Wild blueberry picking in the region's blueberry fields (in season, mid-July to mid-August): lowbush blueberry fields stretch as far as the eye can see, covering the ground in a blue-purple carpet. Lac-Saint-Jean blueberries are smaller and more flavorful than cultivated blueberries—they're used to make pies, jams, wines, and local liqueurs.

days_plan.itin_title: Saguenay Fjord & Lac-Saint-Jean Loop

visits.text: Place Royale, in the Lower Town, marks the exact spot where Samuel de Champlain built his first habitation on July 3, 1608. Notre-Dame-des-Victoires church, erected in 1688, is the oldest stone church in Canada. The gabled houses bordering the square, with steep roofs and stone walls, evoke a Norman village. Champlain chose this site for the river's narrowing, which offered a natural defensive advantage—"Kebec" means "where the river narrows" in Algonquin.

The Petit-Champlain quarter, at the cliff's base, is linked to the Upper Town by the funicular (inaugurated 1879) or the Breakneck Stairs. Its cobbled streets, among North America's oldest, shelter artisan boutiques, leather workshops, and cafés. In winter, decorations transform the quarter into a Christmas village.

Château Frontenac, designed by American architect Bruce Price for the Canadian Pacific Railway Company, opened in 1893. Built on the very site of Champlain's fort and Château Saint-Louis, it was expanded several times: the Citadel wing in 1899, the Mont-Carmel wing in 1908, the central tower in 1924. In 1943, Churchill and Roosevelt met here to plan the Normandy landings. Dufferin Terrace, a long wooden boardwalk alongside it, overlooks the St. Lawrence River from 60 meters up.

Québec's fortifications form 4.6 kilometers of ramparts with gates, cannons, and towers. Québec is the only walled city in North America north of Mexico whose walls remain intact. The first palisades date from Champlain in 1608. The current ramparts were built between 1693 and 1871, blending French (Vauban-style) and British influences. The Citadel, built between 1820 and 1850, is the largest British fortress in North America; it's still occupied by the Royal 22e Régiment, the Canadian Armed Forces' only francophone regiment.

Montmorency Falls plunges 83 meters into the St. Lawrence River—30 meters taller than Niagara Falls. Fed by the Montmorency River cascading down the Laurentian escarpment, it bears the name of the Duke of Montmorency, viceroy of New France in the 17th century. A suspension bridge at the summit crosses the spray curtain. In winter, spray freezes at the falls' base to form an ice cone called "pain de sucre" (sugar loaf), which Québécois have been climbing since the 19th century.

Île d'Orléans, connected to the mainland by a single bridge since 1935, is a repository of rural Québec. Six parishes founded in the 17th century (the oldest, Sainte-Famille, dates from 1661) line a 67-kilometer road circling the island. Maple groves, apple orchards, strawberry fields, and vineyards compose an agricultural landscape barely changed since the French regime. The island counts over 600 heritage buildings, some from the 18th century.

Sainte-Anne-de-Beaupré Basilica, a pilgrimage site since 1658, draws hundreds of thousands of visitors annually. The current Romanesque Revival building dates from 1926—the two previous basilicas were destroyed by fire. Its facade is flanked by two 91-meter steeples. Inside, 240 stained-glass windows recount the life of Saint Anne. Crutches and prosthetics hanging at the entrance testify to healings pilgrims attribute to the saint.

activities.text: Island tour by car or bicycle, with stops at farms to taste ice cider, artisan cheeses, and homemade preserves. Producers open their doors from May to October.

places.name: Regina

visits.text: Stop in Moose Jaw, halfway between Winnipeg and Regina: beneath downtown streets, a network of underground tunnels served as refuge for Chinese immigrants persecuted by early 20th-century exclusion laws. These passages, discovered in the 1980s, are now recreated as museum tours.

Wascana Centre in the heart of Regina spreads across 930 hectares around an artificial lake—one of Canada's largest urban parks. The Saskatchewan Legislative Building, constructed between 1908 and 1912 in Beaux-Arts style, reflects in the calm waters of Wascana Lake. On its lawns in 1935, the tragic end of the On-to-Ottawa Trek unfolded when thousands of unemployed men heading to the federal capital were stopped by police in a bloody riot.

The RCMP Heritage Centre traces the history of the Royal Canadian Mounted Police since its creation in 1873. Regina is the RCMP's only training center in Canada: all cadets pass through here. The museum displays uniforms, equipment, and accounts of the first mounted patrols across the Western Prairies.

activities.text: Long driving day across the Manitoba and Saskatchewan prairies (490 km): straight roads to the horizon, canola and wheat fields, grain elevators punctuating the skyline.

days_plan.itin_title: Cross-Canada Journey: From Québec City to the Rockies

places.name: Revelstoke

visits.text: The Trans-Canada Highway runs east through Vernon and Eagle Pass. At Craigellachie, a monument commemorates the Last Spike of the Canadian Pacific Railway, driven on November 7, 1885, at 9:22 a.m. by financier Donald Smith. This act sealed the transcontinental railway link of 5,000 km connecting Montreal to the Pacific—a promise made to British Columbia to join Confederation in 1871. Smith missed his first swing and bent the spike; he had it straightened and cut into jewellery for the wives of the dignitaries present.

Arrival in Revelstoke, a small mountain town nestled at the confluence of the Columbia and Illecillewaet Rivers, between the Selkirk and Monashee Mountains. The historic downtown preserves early-20th-century facades from the era when Revelstoke served as a dispatching centre for the Canadian Pacific.

Mount Revelstoke National Park, created in 1914 at the initiative of the residents themselves, is the only Canadian national park whose summit you can reach by car. The Meadows in the Sky Parkway, a 26-km road built between 1911 and 1927, climbs 1,400 metres in elevation—from temperate rainforest cedars to alpine meadows ablaze with wildflowers in August. At the summit (1,930 m), views sweep across the Monashee and Selkirk ranges.
The Giant Cedars Boardwalk, a 500-metre walkway along the Trans-Canada Highway, traverses an inland temperate rainforest of red cedars and hemlocks, some exceeding 800 years old.

Glacier National Park (1,349 km², created in 1886) protects Rogers Pass, at 1,330 m elevation, discovered in 1881 by Major Albert Bowman Rogers for the Canadian Pacific. The pass receives an average of 14 metres of snow per year. Avalanches killed 200 workers in 30 years, forcing the excavation of the Connaught Tunnel (8 km) in 1916.

places.name: Rimouski

visits.text: The Pointe-au-Père Maritime Historic Site unfolds around three focal points. The Empress of Ireland Museum chronicles the sinking of May 29, 1914: in just 14 minutes, the Canadian Pacific liner went down after being rammed by the Norwegian collier Storstad in thick fog, taking 1,012 of the 1,477 people aboard with her. This remains Canada's worst maritime disaster, though it was quickly overshadowed by the outbreak of World War I two months later. The wreck still rests at 45 meters depth off the coast of Sainte-Luce.

The HMCS Onondaga submarine, an Oberon-class vessel, stretches 90 meters long. Launched in 1965 and decommissioned in 2000, it's the only submarine in Canada open to visitors. You descend through narrow hatches into the vessel's belly to discover the torpedo room, command post, and stacked bunks where 70 submariners slept.

The Pointe-au-Père Lighthouse, built in 1909, is an octagonal reinforced concrete tower standing 33 meters tall—one of Canada's tallest lighthouses. It guided navigation through the St. Lawrence estuary for nearly a century. Climb the stairs for panoramic views of the river, which spans 40 km wide at this point.

The Musée régional de Rimouski occupies eastern Quebec's oldest stone church, built in 1824. Exhibitions alternate between contemporary art, science, and regional history of the Bas-Saint-Laurent. The building itself, with its neoclassical façade, is worth the visit.

Maison Lamontagne, built in 1744, is designated a national heritage building. It's one of the rare surviving examples of New France architecture still standing in the region. Its construction blends colombage pierroté (half-timbering with stone infill) and pièce sur pièce (horizontal log construction), both typical techniques of the colonial era.

The Matapédia Valley, taken on the return route between Carleton and Rimouski, is a glacial valley carved between the Notre-Dame Mountains. The Matapédia River, 73 km long, is one of Quebec's most renowned for Atlantic salmon fishing. Route 132 then Route 6 wind between dense forests and forestry villages.

The Canyon des Portes de l'Enfer in Saint-Narcisse-de-Rimouski takes its name from the deep gorges carved by the Rimouski River. The suspension bridge spanning the canyon peaks at 63 meters height—Quebec's highest suspension bridge. A 300-step staircase, baptized 'la descente aux enfers' (the descent into hell), reaches the river level below. The trails also pass by the Grand Sault waterfall and a tribute to the log drivers, those men who floated timber down the river in the forestry era.

activities.text: Stroll along the Promenade de la Mer at sunset. Rimouski was named Quebec's 'City of Happiness' in 2013, and its estuary sunsets rank among the province's most spectacular.

Cross by kayak or zodiac to Île Saint-Barnabé, a 4-km-long island facing Rimouski. A hermit named Toussaint Cartier lived there alone from 1728 to 1767. Hiking trails traverse the island through forest and shoreline.
Hike at Canyon des Portes de l'Enfer: several hiking trails offer 2 to 12 km of routes between lookouts, bridges, and waterfalls. Crossing the 63-meter suspension bridge is the highlight.
Sunset over the river from the Rimouski promenade, to close this Gaspésie loop.
