places.name: Riviere Eternite

visits.text: Baie-Éternité sector of Saguenay Fjord National Park (328 km², established 1983): this is where the fjord reaches its most spectacular dimensions. Two rocky capes face each other across the water: Cap Trinité (518 m) and Cap Éternité (457 m). Their Precambrian rock walls plunge almost vertically into the fjord, which reaches its maximum depth of 275 meters here.

Hike the Statue Trail (7.8 km round-trip, 350 m elevation gain, moderate to difficult): the trail climbs through boreal forest to the summit of Cap Trinité, where the statue of Notre-Dame-du-Saguenay stands. This statue made of pine covered with lead sheets, 7.5 meters tall, was installed in 1881 at the cliff's edge. It was commissioned by Charles-Napoléon Robitaille, a traveling salesman from Chicoutimi who had promised to erect it if he survived nearly drowning in the Saguenay's icy waters. Sculptor Louis Jobin created the work in three blocks of white pine, hollowed out to reduce weight. Hoisting it up the cliff face—cut into 14 pieces, raised 180 meters above the water using pulleys and beams—took eight days.

The Saguenay Fjord stretches approximately 105 km between Saint-Fulgence and Tadoussac. It's the southernmost navigable fjord in North America and one of the world's longest. Its waters have a unique feature: a thin layer of fresh water (7% of the volume) from Lac Saint-Jean floats atop a mass of salt water that entered from the Atlantic via the St. Lawrence River. This stratification creates a unique ecosystem frequented by about 900 belugas that regularly visit the fjord and its mouth (the entire St. Lawrence estuary population numbers about 1,700 individuals).

activities.text: Boat or zodiac cruise on the fjord from Baie-Éternité: navigating at the base of Caps Trinité and Éternité gives you the true measure of these 300-meter cliffs. Your gaze travels upward endlessly. In summer, seals sometimes lounge on rocks at water level.

days_plan.itin_title: Saguenay Fjord & Lac-Saint-Jean Loop

map_keywords: Sentier de la Statue Cap Trinité

visits.text: Arrival in Chicoutimi, one of three boroughs that form the City of Saguenay (along with Jonquière and La Baie). La Pulperie de Chicoutimi: built between 1896 and 1930, this former pulp mill was once the world's largest. The complex of brick and stone buildings along the Chicoutimi River has been converted into a regional museum showcasing industrial history, contemporary Québécois art, and the house of Arthur Villeneuve—a barber-turned-painter who transformed his entire home into a work of art by covering every surface—walls, ceiling, floors—with naïve paintings.

La Petite Maison Blanche (The Little White House): this modest wooden home became a symbol of resilience after the July 1996 Saguenay Flood. For 50 hours, torrential rains dumped approximately 270 mm of water on the region, triggering floods and landslides that killed 10 people, forced the evacuation of 16,000, and caused $1.5 billion in damage. Everything around the Little White House was swept away—but it stood firm, alone in the middle of devastated ground. It's now a museum dedicated to the disaster.

Rue Racine, Chicoutimi's main downtown artery: restaurants, cafés, art galleries, and local shops. This is the beating heart of urban Saguenay, with its summer terraces and colorful storefronts. Chicoutimi takes its name from the Innu word 'Ishkutimiu,' meaning 'up to where it is deep'—a reference to the Saguenay River, which becomes navigable at this point.

activities.text: Evening at Saguenay's microbreweries: the region boasts several craft breweries that highlight boreal ingredients—beers brewed with wild blueberries, boreal forest herbs, or local honey.

days_plan.itin_title: Saguenay Fjord & Lac-Saint-Jean Loop

visits.text: Val-Jalbert Historic Village: an authentic ghost town, not a reconstruction. In 1901, Damase Jalbert founded a pulp mill here at the foot of Ouiatchouan Falls, whose hydraulic power ran the machinery. An entire village grew around it: workers' houses, general store, school, convent, hotel. At its peak, about 80 families lived here. When the mill closed in 1927—due to falling pulp prices and competition—everyone left. The buildings remained as they were, frozen in time. Today you walk through the streets of a 1920s village, past houses with empty windows and a ruined mill.

Ouiatchouan Falls, in the heart of Val-Jalbert: 72 meters high—20 meters taller than Niagara Falls. The name comes from the Innu 'Ouiatchouan,' meaning 'rapids with white foam.' A cable car or a 764-step staircase leads to the summit, where the view sweeps across Lac Saint-Jean and the village below. A glass-floor viewing platform lets you stand above the void, right in the heart of the cascade. Upstream, a second waterfall (Maligne Falls, 49 m) is accessible via trail.

Mashteuiatsh (formerly Pointe-Bleue): community of the Pekuakamiulnuatsh First Nation, on Lac Saint-Jean's western shore. It's the region's only Indigenous community. The Ilnu Museum, founded in 1977, traces more than 6,000 years of Pekuakamiulnuatsh history—the Innu people of Lac Saint-Jean. The permanent exhibition 'Tshilanu Ilnuatsh' (We, the Ilnuatsh), winner of a Canadian Museums Association Award of Excellence, explores culture and way of life through the seasons, guided by the voices of Kukum (grandmother) and Mushum (grandfather).

Zoo sauvage de Saint-Félicien (485 hectares): a zoo that reverses the roles. The 'Sentiers de la nature' trail is experienced aboard a small mesh-enclosed train that crosses vast enclosures of caribou, black bears, wolves, bison, and wapiti—visitors are 'caged,' animals roam semi-free in natural habitats. More than 85 species, mainly boreal, coexist in this zoo that presents itself as a living museum of Canadian Shield fauna.

activities.text: Tasting of Lac-Saint-Jean regional products: artisan cheeses (including the famous Perron cheddar, made in Saint-Prime since 1890), tourtière du Lac-Saint-Jean (a slow-cooked meat pie different from tourtière elsewhere in Québec), and local microbrewery beers.

days_plan.itin_title: Saguenay Fjord & Lac-Saint-Jean Loop

visits.text: Sainte-Rose-du-Nord, nicknamed 'the pearl of the fjord': this village of about 400 is tucked into a narrow cove, framed by two forested rocky capes. You reach it via a winding road that drops sharply toward the fjord. The small Nature Museum displays collections of rocks, minerals, and natural specimens from the region. The observation wharf offers a direct view of the fjord's dark waters and the cliffs on the opposite shore.

Parc Aventures Cap Jaseux in Saint-Fulgence: an outdoor adventure site perched on the fjord cliffs. The park offers a via ferrata (climbing route on rock face with cables and rungs bolted to the rock), unusual suspended accommodations in the trees (treehouses and glass domes), and sea kayaking on the fjord. The site overlooks the Saguenay directly—the views from the aerial routes are dizzying.

Saint-Fulgence marks the fjord's western end—this is where the Saguenay River ceases to be a fjord and becomes an ordinary river. For experienced hikers, Monts-Valin National Park is accessible 45 minutes north. In winter, its summit is famous for the 'Valley of the Ghosts': frost and snow-covered trees take on spectral shapes.

activities.text: Sea kayaking at sunset on the fjord from Cap Jaseux: paddle between the fjord's rocky walls as the low-angle light paints the cliffs in orange hues. The silence is broken only by the sound of paddles and the cry of gulls.

days_plan.itin_title: Saguenay Fjord & Lac-Saint-Jean Loop

places.name: Sainte Anne Des Monts

visits.text: Gaspésie National Park, established in 1937, protects 802 km² in the heart of the peninsula. It contains more than 25 peaks exceeding 1,000 meters, including 7 of Quebec's 10 highest. The Chic-Choc Mountains—from the Mi'kmaq word meaning 'impassable barrier'—form a 95-km-long range parallel to the river. Mont Albert, at 1,154 meters, is composed of serpentine, a rock usually buried in the Earth's mantle and rarely exposed at the surface. Its summit plateau harbors alpine tundra with Arctic plants, at a latitude where you'd normally find boreal forest.

Lac aux Américains is a glacial cirque formed about 10,000 years ago, nestled at the foot of steep walls. The 2.4-km trail (round-trip) crosses a boreal forest of balsam firs before opening onto this natural basin of dark waters. The name recalls the Americans who came to fish for Atlantic salmon in the Sainte-Anne River in the 19th century.

The Martre Lighthouse, en route to Sainte-Anne-des-Monts, is one of Quebec's rare wooden lighthouses. Painted red, it was built in 1906 and designated a heritage building. It dominates the Haute-Gaspésie shoreline and houses a small interpretation center on St. Lawrence lighthouses.

activities.text: Ascent of Mont Albert: the 11.6-km loop with 860 meters of elevation gain crosses boreal forest, then alpine tundra. At the summit, the serpentine plateau offers panoramic views of the Chic-Chocs. The park was originally created to protect the Gaspésie caribou herd—estimated at fewer than 50 individuals—the last remaining south of the St. Lawrence.
For a more accessible option, the Mont Ernest-Laforce trail (5.2 km loop) offers viewpoints over valleys and summits without the difficulty of alpine ascent.

days_plan.itin_title: Gaspésie Loop: From Rimouski to the End of the World

map_keywords: Parc national de la Gaspésie Sainte-Anne-des-Monts

places.name: Sault Ste Marie

visits.text: Sault Ste. Marie Canal National Historic Site: completed in 1895, this canal was the world's longest lock and the first to operate on electricity. It's the final link in the all-Canadian navigation route connecting the Atlantic to Lake Superior. The rapids of the St. Marys River—called Baawaating ("place of the rapids") in Anishinaabemowin—served as a fishing and trading center for Indigenous peoples for millennia. The historic red sandstone buildings were cut from stone excavated during canal construction.

Stroll the boardwalk along the St. Marys River: from this promenade, you can watch freighters transit between Lake Huron and Lake Superior via the American locks (four US locks, still in commercial operation). The 6-meter elevation difference between the two lakes creates the rapids that give the city its name—"sault" meaning "rapid" in old French.

activities.text: Scenic drive from North Bay via Sudbury and the Trans-Canada Highway (Route 17), a full day's drive through the boreal forest of the Canadian Shield.

days_plan.itin_title: Cross-Canada Journey: From Québec City to the Rockies

Bushplane Heritage Centre Sault Ste. Marie

places.name: Shediac

visits.text: Shediac, self-proclaimed "Lobster Capital of the World": an 11-metre giant lobster statue marks the town entrance. The Northumberland Strait's current-warmed waters make this among the warmest beaches north of Virginia. Parlee Beach, with its fine sand and shallow waters, attracts families from across Atlantic Canada. Each summer, Shediac's Lobster Festival celebrates the king of Maritime crustaceans.

Bouctouche, 50 km north of Shediac: this Acadian village houses Le Pays de la Sagouine, a cultural park inspired by the character created by Acadian writer Antonine Maillet (1979 Prix Goncourt). On an island reached by footbridge, costumed actors bring to life everyday Acadian life in the 1930s-40s—a world of lobster fishing, evening gatherings, and quiet resistance. The Bouctouche Dune, a 12 km sandbar jutting into the bay, is one of northeastern America's last major dune systems.

activities.text: Lobster tasting on Shediac wharf: fishermen sell their daily catch directly. Maritime lobster, caught in cold Atlantic waters, is renowned for sweet, tender meat.

days_plan.itin_title: Canadian Maritimes: Halifax to Cape Breton Loop

map_keywords: Bouctouche Le Pays de la Sagouine

visits.text: Drive to Tadoussac on Route 138 along the river. The road crosses Charlevoix villages (Saint-Siméon, Baie-des-Rochers) with glimpses of the ever-widening St. Lawrence. At Baie-Sainte-Catherine, a free ferry crosses the Saguenay River in 10 minutes—the only road access to Tadoussac. From the ferry deck, view the fjord mouth, less than a kilometer wide here. With luck, you'll spot belugas from the boat.

Tadoussac, a village of 800, occupies one of the world's most beautiful bays (member of the Club of the Most Beautiful Bays in the World since 1998). Jacques Cartier anchored here in 1535. In 1600, Pierre Chauvin founded New France's first fur trading post—eight years before Champlain founded Québec City. A reconstruction of the trading post, facing the beach, traces the history of exchanges with First Nations.

The Chapelle des Indiens (1747) is Canada's oldest surviving wooden chapel, with its red roof and white walls. Hôtel Tadoussac, recognizable by its large red roof, has welcomed visitors since 1864—the first tourists arrived by steamboat from Montréal and Québec. The Tadoussac Dunes, 5 kilometers south of the village, form a striking landscape of sand and forest bordering the river.

The Marine Mammal Interpretation Centre (CIMM), run by the Group for Research and Education on Marine Mammals (GREMM), presents the 13 cetacean species frequenting the St. Lawrence estuary. Life-size skeletons include a 13-meter sperm whale. Centre naturalists individually track humpback whales and belugas, identifying them by skin or fluke markings.

Saguenay Fjord National Park protects the shores of the world's southernmost glacial fjord. Carved by glaciers 10,000 years ago, the Saguenay reaches 276 meters depth at Cap Éternité but only 20 meters at its mouth—glacial debris having formed a natural sill. At Baie Éternité, the cliffs of Cap Trinité rise over 300 meters sheer above the fjord's black water. The Statue trail (3.5 km, 1h30) leads to a 9-meter Virgin Mary statue installed in 1881 atop the cliff.

activities.text: Observation cruise from Les Escoumins

Whale-watching cruise in the St. Lawrence estuary at the Saguenay confluence. The meeting of the Saguenay's cold, fresh water with the river's saltwater creates upwellings that bring krill and small fish to the surface, attracting cetaceans. From May to October, you can observe minke whales, fin whales (second-largest animal on Earth), humpback whales, and occasionally blue whales. St. Lawrence belugas, a resident population of roughly 1,700, frequent the area year-round.

days_plan.itin_title: Eastern Canada Road Trip: Toronto, Niagara Falls & Québec City

places.name: Thunder Bay

visits.text: From Hillcrest Park or the Terry Fox Memorial, observe the Sleeping Giant—a formation of diabase mesas on the Sibley Peninsula that traces the silhouette of a giant lying on his back. According to Ojibwe legend, it's Nanabijou, spirit of the deep waters, turned to stone for revealing the location of a silver mine to Europeans. The cliffs rank among Ontario's highest (250 m).

The Terry Fox Memorial on the Trans-Canada Highway east of Thunder Bay marks where the young runner had to stop his Marathon of Hope on September 1, 1980, after 143 days and 5,373 km running on an artificial leg. The bronze statue, facing west—the direction he never reached—overlooks Lake Superior.

Fort William Historical Park recreates the continent's largest inland fur trading post as it was in 1816. Forty-two buildings bring to life the North West Company depot, where voyageurs, traders, and First Nations gathered each summer to exchange furs, provisions, and news.

activities.text: Hike to the top of the Sleeping Giant (Top of the Giant Trail): 22 km round trip, panoramic views of Lake Superior from the cliffs of the Sibley Peninsula.

places.name: Tofino

visits.text: Drive Whistler to Horseshoe Bay, then ferry crossing to Nanaimo (1h40 sailing across the Strait of Georgia). Route 4 (Pacific Rim Highway) then crosses Vancouver Island from east to west.

Stop at Cathedral Grove (MacMillan Provincial Park) on Route 4 between Parksville and Port Alberni. This grove of giant Douglas firs, some exceeding 800 years of age and 75 metres in height with 9-metre circumferences, is one of the last accessible remnants of Vancouver Island's old-growth forest. A 300-metre trail winds between the massive trunks. The section between Port Alberni and Tofino is winding but spectacular, skirting the shores of Kennedy Lake. Evening arrival in Tofino.

Pacific Rim National Park protects Long Beach, 16 km of grey sand pounded by Pacific waves, backed by temperate rainforest. The spray-laden air, beached tree trunks, and patrolling bald eagles compose an intact wilderness landscape.

Meares Island, accessible by water taxi from Tofino (10 minutes), harbours the Big Tree Trail, a boardwalk path winding between red cedars over 1,000 years old, some reaching 4 metres in diameter. The island has been protected since 1984 thanks to mobilization by the Tla-o-qui-aht Nation, which opposed logging by declaring the island a tribal park—one of the first victories of the First Nations environmental movement in British Columbia.

activities.text: Surf lessons in Tofino

Surf or watch the waves at Cox Bay or Chesterman Beach. Tofino is Canada's surf capital thanks to its consistent waves and sandy bottom. In autumn and winter, Pacific storms generate spectacular swells—a local tradition is "storm watching" from the beaches or accommodation terraces.

days_plan.itin_title: Western Canada Road Trip: Rockies and Pacific Coast
Western Canada Road Trip: Rockies and Pacific Coast

map_keywords: Long Beach Pacific Rim National Park
Meares Island Big Tree Trail Tofino

visits.text: Kensington Market traces its roots to waves of Jewish, Portuguese, and Caribbean immigrants who transformed Victorian row houses into a sprawling open-air market. Its rainbow-painted homes now shelter vintage clothing shops, global groceries, and fiercely independent cafés. Adjacent Chinatown stretches along Spadina Avenue with herbalist shops, dim sum parlors, and Mandarin signage—one of North America's largest Chinese quarters thriving since the 1870s.

Graffiti Alley runs for over a kilometer between Portland Street and Bathurst Street, cutting through the heart of Queen West. Every wall wears monumental murals refreshed annually by local and international street artists. Continue onto Queen Street West itself, Toronto's bohemian artery lined with galleries, vinyl record shops, and boutiques showcasing Canadian designers.

The Distillery District occupies the former Gooderham & Worts distillery, founded in 1832 by two English immigrants. By the 1860s, this operation was producing over two million gallons of whisky annually—the largest distillery in the British Empire. Shuttered in 1990, purchased in 2001, it reopened in 2003 as a pedestrian quarter: more than 40 Victorian red-brick buildings, designated a National Historic Site, now house art galleries, glassblowing studios, and microbreweries.

The CN Tower soars 553 meters above Lake Ontario. Built between 1973 and 1976 by Canadian National to solve radio interference caused by downtown skyscrapers, it held the title of world's tallest freestanding structure for over 30 years. More than 1,500 workers labored for 40 months. The Sikorsky helicopter "Olga" hoisted the antenna's 39 sections to the summit. The glass floor at 342 meters suspends visitors above the void. On clear days, the view reaches into New York State.

Catch the ferry at Jack Layton Terminal (departures every 15 minutes in season) to the Toronto Islands. Ward's Island preserves a cottage village inhabited year-round since 1880, with gardens and shaded pathways. Centre Island offers beaches facing Lake Ontario and unobstructed skyline views. The archipelago forms a 4-kilometer chain of 15 entirely car-free islands.

The Royal Ontario Museum, founded in 1912, houses over 13 million objects. Its modern crystal facade of aluminum and glass—the "Michael Lee-Chin Crystal"—was added in 2007. Collections of ancient Chinese art, dinosaur fossils, and minerals rank among the world's finest. Alternative: the Art Gallery of Ontario, redesigned by Frank Gehry in 2008, featuring a 180-meter wood-and-glass galleria suspended above the street.

activities.text: Street food tasting through Kensington Market: tacos, Jamaican patties, empanadas, and fresh-pressed juices from vendors lining the narrow streets.

Evening in Koreatown along Bloor Street West between Christie and Bathurst. Korean barbecue restaurants, karaoke bars, and soju lounges line several blocks. Alternative: Little Italy on College Street for sidewalk terraces and espresso cafés.

Eastern Canada Road Trip: Toronto, Niagara Falls & Québec City

visits.text: Gastown, Vancouver's oldest neighbourhood, takes its name from "Gassy" Jack Deighton, a former steamboat captain born in Hull, Yorkshire, who arrived in September 1867 with just six dollars in his pocket, his Indigenous wife, and a yellow dog. Legend has it he promised sawmill workers all the whisky they could drink in exchange for their help—24 hours later, a saloon stood complete. The district, devastated by the Great Fire of 1886, was rebuilt in brick and stone. Designated a National Historic Site in 2009, it retains its original cobblestones and Victorian facades. At the corner of Water and Cambie Streets, the 1977 steam clock (designed by horologist Raymond Saunders) runs partly on steam from the city's underground district heating system and partly on electric motors: every quarter-hour, its brass whistles play the Westminster Chimes, and a plume of steam shoots from the top on the hour.

Stanley Park covers 405 hectares of temperate rainforest on a peninsula—nearly 20% larger than New York's Central Park. Opened September 27, 1888, and named after Governor General Frederick Stanley, it was designated a National Historic Site in 1988. The seawall, an 8.8-km promenade, circles the park along the ocean. At Brockton Point, nine totem poles carved by First Nations artists (Squamish, Haida, Kwakwaka'wakw) stand in a clearing—the collection began in the 1920s when the Parks Board acquired four totems from Alert Bay. The current poles, moved to Brockton Point in 1962, are mostly red cedar replicas of originals preserved in museums.

English Bay and Sunset Beach complete the park's western loop along Vancouver's most popular urban beaches. English Bay Beach, facing due west, offers Pacific sunsets with the mountains of Vancouver Island as backdrop.

Granville Island occupies a 14-hectare artificial peninsula created in 1915 by dredging False Creek to accommodate heavy industry. Abandoned in the 1970s, the site was rehabilitated and the Public Market opened July 12, 1979, with 21 vendors. The painted corrugated-metal buildings preserve their original beams and a ten-tonne hoist hanging from the ceiling, remnants of the former cable factory. Today the market hosts about fifty fresh-produce merchants.

The Kitsilano neighbourhood, once a 1960s counterculture stronghold, stretches between Kits Beach and streets lined with bungalows. Kitsilano Pool, 137 metres long, is Canada's largest saltwater outdoor pool.

Free morning in Victoria: Fisherman's Wharf, a collection of colourful floating homes and seafood stalls, makes a final stop before departure. The James Bay neighbourhood, Victoria's oldest, preserves Victorian and Edwardian houses along chestnut-lined streets.

Ferry Victoria to Vancouver (Swartz Bay to Tsawwassen, 1h35 crossing of the Strait of Georgia). The sailing passes between the Gulf Islands, an archipelago of small wooded islands with a Mediterranean microclimate. Afternoon arrival in Vancouver.

activities.text: Walk or cycle the Stanley Park seawall, a flat 8.8-km loop passing Prospect Point (with views of Lions Gate Bridge), Siwash Rock, and Third Beach.

Cross the Capilano Suspension Bridge in North Vancouver, a 137-metre footbridge suspended 70 metres above the Capilano River. Originally built in 1889 with hemp ropes and cedar planks by Scottish real-estate developer George Grant Mackay, it has been rebuilt four times since. The park also features the Treetops Adventure (seven suspension bridges in the canopy 33 metres above ground) and the Cliffwalk (a 213-metre cantilevered walkway bolted to the granite cliff face). Free alternative: Lynn Canyon Suspension Bridge, lower but set in pristine nature.
Cross the Capilano Suspension Bridge with views over the Capilano River Regional Park forest

visits.text: Return drive to Vancouver Island's east coast (about 4 hours). Victoria's Inner Harbour is dominated by the British Columbia Parliament Buildings, completed in 1898 by architect Francis Rattenbury in a neo-Baroque style. The edifice, whose facade measures 150 metres, is illuminated by over 3,000 lights at night. Facing the harbour, the same Rattenbury designed the Empress Hotel (1908), a grand château-style hotel that hosts a traditional afternoon tea.

Craigdarroch Castle, a 39-room Victorian mansion built between 1887 and 1890 for Robert Dunsmuir, a coal baron who was then British Columbia's wealthiest man. He died before construction was completed. The interior preserves its oak panelling, original stained glass, and an 87-step staircase leading to the tower from which views extend to the Olympic Mountains of Washington State.

Beacon Hill Park, 75 hectares of gardens and meadows facing the Strait of Juan de Fuca. Here stands the Mile Zero marker of the Trans-Canada Highway—the 7,821-km highway linking Victoria to St. John's, Newfoundland.

activities.text: Whale-watching safari off Victoria

visits.text: Riding Mountain National Park (2,969 km², officially established 1933) stands atop the Manitoba Escarpment, 400 meters above the surrounding prairies. It's Canada's only national park protecting three ecosystems converging in one place: grasslands, boreal forest, and eastern deciduous forest. The East Gate, a national historic site, is a monumental log-and-stone entrance built in the 1930s by 1,200 workers as part of Great Depression relief programs.

Walk along Clear Lake from the village of Wasagaming: this crystalline lake, 34 meters deep, is the recreational heart of the park. The village retains the charm of a 1930s resort town, with its log buildings and period theatre.

The Lake Audy bison enclosure shelters a herd of about forty plains bison in 500 hectares of native prairie. These bison recall the immense herds that once covered the Canadian Prairies before their near-extinction in the 19th century.

activities.text: Hike the Grey Owl Trail (8.9 km one way) to Grey Owl's cabin, built in 1931 beside Beaver Lodge Lake. Archibald Belaney, a Briton who reinvented himself under the Indigenous identity of Grey Owl, was one of Canada's first conservationists. Hired by Parks Canada to reintroduce beavers, he lived six months in this L-shaped log cabin with his two beavers, Jelly Roll and Rawhide.

places.name: Wawa

visits.text: The Agawa Rock pictographs in Lake Superior Provincial Park (1,550 km², established 1944): on a white crystalline granite cliff rising 15 stories high, 35 red ochre paintings created by Ojibwe spiritual leaders between the 17th and 18th centuries. The most famous depicts Mishipeshu, the Great Lynx of the water, guardian of copper. The access trail (800 m) descends between rock crevices to a ledge on the lake's edge—accessible only in calm weather.

Stop at Old Woman Bay, a cove framed by sheer cliffs plunging directly into Lake Superior. This region of Algoma is where the Group of Seven—those 1920s Canadian painters—created some of their most celebrated canvases. The landscape of twisted pines, pink rock, and deep blue water resembles a living painting.

activities.text: Hike the Nokomis Trail or the coastal trail in Lake Superior Provincial Park, through boreal forest on the Canadian Shield.

visits.text: Long driving day via Kamloops (junction of the North and South Thompson Rivers) then the Sea-to-Sky Highway (Route 99) snaking between the fjords of Howe Sound and the peaks of the Coast Mountains. Evening arrival in Whistler. The pedestrian village, whose ski resort opened in 1966 and whose development accelerated with the 2010 Winter Olympics (Sea-to-Sky Highway upgrade, construction of sports venues), lines up its stone-and-wood chalets at the foot of Whistler (2,182 m) and Blackcomb (2,440 m) Mountains.
Hike around Garibaldi Lake
Stroll through Whistler Village and relax in spas

activities.text: Kayak or paddleboard on Whistler's lakes

Hike to Garibaldi Lake, a turquoise lake nestled at 1,484 metres elevation in a volcanic cirque of Garibaldi Provincial Park. The trail climbs through dense forest of firs and hemlocks before opening onto the lake, held back by a natural lava barrier called The Barrier.

Alternative: ride the Peak 2 Peak Gondola, a cable car linking Whistler and Blackcomb Mountains over 4.4 km—a world record for unsupported span at the time of its 2008 construction. The cabin completes the journey in 11 minutes, suspended 415 metres above Fitzsimmons Creek valley.
Kayak or paddleboard on Lost Lake, a small forest lake accessible on foot from Whistler Village, surrounded by hiking trails and developed beaches.

places.name: Winnipeg

visits.text: The Forks, at the confluence of the Red and Assiniboine rivers: a gathering place, trading center, and site of peace treaties for Indigenous peoples for over 6,000 years. Archaeological excavations have uncovered nearly 400,000 artifacts, some dating back to 1100 CE. Today, the indoor market and riverside docks animate this national historic site.

The Canadian Museum for Human Rights, adjacent to The Forks: opened in 2014, designed by architect Antoine Predock, it's the first national museum built outside the federal capital and the world's first dedicated to human rights. Its 11 galleries are connected by alabaster ramps. The cornerstone comes from Runnymede, England, where the Magna Carta was sealed in 1215.

The Exchange District, north of Portage Avenue: some twenty blocks of brick and stone buildings constructed between 1880 and 1920, forming one of North America's best-preserved assemblages of early 20th-century commercial architecture. These streets witnessed the Winnipeg General Strike of 1919—six weeks of total paralysis, 30,000 strikers, and a Mounted Police charge on June 21 ("Bloody Saturday") that left two dead and marked the history of the Canadian labor movement.

Cross the Provencher Bridge to Saint-Boniface, Western Canada's oldest francophone neighborhood. Saint-Boniface Cathedral, whose 1908 stone facade survived the 1968 fire, stands in ruins framing the new church. This is where Louis Riel, Métis leader and father of Manitoba, is buried in the parish cemetery.

activities.text: Evening at The Forks Market: stalls of Indigenous, Métis, and multicultural cuisine in one of the Prairies' liveliest markets.

places.name: Wolfville

visits.text: Grand-Pré, a National Historic Site and UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2012: here in the Annapolis Valley unfolded one of Canadian history's darkest chapters. Founded around 1680 by Acadian families from Port-Royal, Grand-Pré became Acadia's breadbasket through a network of dikes that transformed salt marshes into fertile farmland. On September 5, 1755, Colonel John Winslow assembled all males over age 10 in the church and read the deportation order. Some 2,200 Acadians were forcibly embarked—a third of the 6,000 deported from Nova Scotia that year. In total, between 1755 and 1763, approximately 10,000 Acadians were scattered across the Atlantic. Some reached Louisiana, where "Acadian" became "Cajun."

Walk through the diked lands surrounding Grand-Pré: these polders, created by Acadians in the 17th century using the aboiteau system (wooden sluices that prevent seawater from rising while draining freshwater), are still farmed today. The memorial church, built in 1922 in the style of old Acadian churches, houses the statue of Evangeline, heroine of Longfellow's epic poem (1847) that made the Acadian tragedy world-famous.

Fort Anne in Annapolis Royal (30 km southwest of Wolfville): Canada's oldest National Historic Site, administered by Parks Canada since 1917. This star-shaped fort with grassy earthen ramparts was the scene of 14 sieges and battles between 1613 and 1710—more than any other site in Canada. This is where Port-Royal stood, capital of French Acadia before its final capture by the British in 1710. The powder magazine museum exhibits four centuries of European presence on this site, from Samuel de Champlain onward.

Annapolis Valley wine route: sheltered by the North and South mountains, the valley enjoys Nova Scotia's mildest microclimate. The apple orchards stretching to the horizon descend from the first plantings of New England settlers (the "Planters") who occupied lands confiscated from Acadians after 1760.

activities.text: Tasting at an Annapolis Valley winery. Gaspereau, Benjamin Bridge, and Luckett vineyards produce cool-climate wines—including Tidal Bay, a white appellation unique to Nova Scotia, created in 2012.
